snapshot.name: Number of transactions in the last 28 days

signals.title: Revenue exceeds MRR by 7.8% — non-recurring items in the mix
signals.detail: 28-day revenue ($4,919) is higher than MRR ($4,562). Likely cause: one-time purchases, non-renewing subscriptions, or annual plan upfront recognition. Be careful — strong revenue here doesn't mean strong recurring base.
signals.caveat: Compare gap over multiple periods to confirm trend.
signals.followup: Pull the Revenue chart segmented by product type to identify the non-recurring contributor.

signals.title: New customers are 55% of active subs in 28d — high acquisition velocity
signals.detail: 1,401 new customers in 28 days against a paid base of 2,536. Either you're growing fast or the paid conversion rate from new → paid is below where it could be. Worth checking conversion-to-paying.
signals.followup: Pull Conversion to Paying chart for the same window.

signals.title: Only 0.45 transactions per new customer in 28d
signals.detail: 627 transactions against 1,401 new customers. Most new customers haven't transacted yet — they're free users. That's expected for freemium, but track conversion-to-paying carefully.

signals.title: Revenue declining across 3 consecutive 28-day periods
signals.detail: Revenue: $5148 → $4984 → $4939. Sustained downtrend across 84 days. Not a single-period dip — this is structural.
signals.followup: Segment revenue by product and store to identify which stream is declining.

signals.title: MRR is flat (-0.0% over 28 days)
signals.detail: Average daily MRR moved from $4540 to $4539 — effectively no change. New subscriptions and churn are likely cancelling each other out. Stagnation this long usually requires a deliberate growth lever (pricing change, new acquisition channel, or conversion optimization).
signals.followup: Pull MRR Movement chart to see if new vs churned are balancing.

signals.title: New trials up 16% vs prior 28 days
signals.detail: 281 new trials in the last 28 days vs 242 prior. Top-of-funnel is expanding. Watch conversion-to-paying to see if this translates to revenue.

signals.title: Churn rate improving (18.7% vs 23.7% prior)
signals.detail: 30-day average churn rate decreased from 23.7% to 18.7%. Retention is strengthening. Good sign — but verify it's not just seasonal.

signals.title: Revenue per subscriber flat for 3 consecutive periods
signals.detail: ARPU (28d revenue / avg active subs): $2.03 → $1.97 → $1.95. Less than 3% movement across 84 days. Pricing power isn't growing. Consider pricing experiments or upsell strategies.
signals.followup: Segment by product duration (monthly vs annual) to check if mix shift is masking ARPU movement.

contradictions.title: MRR is flat (-0.0% over 28 days)
contradictions.detail: Average daily MRR moved from $4540 to $4539 — effectively no change. New subscriptions and churn are likely cancelling each other out. Stagnation this long usually requires a deliberate growth lever (pricing change, new acquisition channel, or conversion optimization).
contradictions.followup: Pull MRR Movement chart to see if new vs churned are balancing.

watchlist.title: New customers are 55% of active subs in 28d — high acquisition velocity
watchlist.detail: 1,401 new customers in 28 days against a paid base of 2,536. Either you're growing fast or the paid conversion rate from new → paid is below where it could be. Worth checking conversion-to-paying.
watchlist.followup: Pull Conversion to Paying chart for the same window.

watchlist.title: 6 chart(s) have incomplete current-period data
watchlist.detail: Charts with incomplete recent values: Revenue, MRR, Active Subscriptions, Active Subscriptions Movement, Active Trials Movement, Churn. The most recent data points in these charts are provisional — they will change as more receipts are processed. Do not base critical decisions on the latest bucket alone.
watchlist.caveat: This is standard RevenueCat behavior. The current period is always incomplete until fully elapsed.

caveats: Charts API data is regenerated from the current snapshot of receipts. Historical values can change when refunds or chargebacks happen.
The current period is always incomplete — comparisons against the most recent bucket are provisional.
Revenue includes one-time purchases and non-renewing subscriptions. It is not a pure proxy for subscription health.
